content:
Member-only story

Angular 22 made signal-first architectures feel obvious. The surprising part was discovering how much rendering work we had been doing without realizing it.

9 min read
Just now

--

A metric we rarely discussed started moving faster than expected. Not latency. Not error rate. Not API throughput. Component render activity.
During a routine Angular 22 migration, our frontend observability dashboards showed something unusual. User traffic was effectively unchanged. Backend response times were unchanged. Infrastructure costs were unchanged.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Yet render activity across some of our busiest pages dropped week after week. By the end of the migration, average component re-renders had fallen by 73%. The migration itself wasn’t particularly difficult. Explaining why the number moved so dramatically took much longer.
What initially looked like an Angular upgrade eventually became an investigation into how state moved through our application. And that investigation exposed several patterns that many teams quietly accumulate as applications grow.
The Dashboard Looked Healthy
